parts.text: Tanha, that desperate craving to control what we cannot control, does not create safety. It creates fragility. The more you shield someone from impermanence, the more impermanence terrifies them. I am Kunal, and I have felt this exact pull — that urge to hold everything still, to keep the people I love small and close and safe, and then the slow painful realization that love sometimes means letting someone walk straight into the storm you survived.

Meera walked into the caravan line without looking back, and Vasuda stood at that gate for three hours after the dust settled. Forty-one days passed. A sandstorm hit Dhanu. Vasuda imagined the worst every single night. Then the caravan returned. Meera climbed down from a camel with cracked lips, sunburned arms, and steadier eyes than Vasuda had ever seen in her own mirror. She had lost weight. She had a scar across her left palm from gripping a rope during a storm.
See you in the next one.
